file_name: Gowthami et al. - 2025 - Performance comparison of deep learning-based classification of skin cancer.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Learning, specifically the NASNet (Neural Architecture Search Network) model, to perform automated skin cancer classification. The study compares NASNet against conventional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset to classify skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work comparing the performance of a proposed NASNet deep learning model against conventional CNNs for skin cancer classification. It includes specific sections on System Methodology, Dataset Details (ISIC dataset), and Experimental Results with performance metrics (accuracy, loss, precision, recall, F1-score). Although it contains a literature review section, its primary purpose is reporting new empirical results from a technical study, and it was published in the proceedings of a conference (ICRDICCT'25).

title: Performance Comparison of Deep Learning-Based Classification of Skin Cancer
journal: In Proceedings of the 1st International Conference on Research and Development in Information, Communication, and Computing Technologies (ICRDICCT‘25 2025)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the performance of a Neural Architecture Search Network (NASNet) for automated skin cancer classification compared to conventional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The researchers utilized the ISIC dataset, comprising 25,000 annotated dermoscopic images, which underwent preprocessing and data augmentation techniques such as rotation and flipping. The NASNet model was designed to automatically optimize feature extraction and classification accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency. Experimental results demonstrated that NASNet surpassed traditional CNNs in terms of classification precision, recall, and F1-score. The findings indicate that NASNet's automated architectural optimization leads to higher validation accuracy and lower loss values during training. Consequently, the study concludes that NASNet is a superior and reliable model for early skin cancer detection and diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the classification of skin cancer using the ISIC dataset, which includes 'a wide range of skin problems, such as benign lesions and malignant types' (Chunk 9). Specific malignant categories mentioned in the introduction are Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), Melanoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) (Chunk 1).

Melanoma is explicitly listed as one of the three biggest categories of skin cancer in the introduction (Chunk 1). It is also mentioned in the literature review regarding detection (Chunk 3, Chunk 6) and in the references (Chunk 12). The ISIC dataset used in the study includes malignant types including melanoma (Chunk 9).
Listed as one of the three biggest categories of skin cancer in the introduction (Chunk 1). The study uses the ISIC dataset which contains malignant types (Chunk 9).
Listed as one of the three biggest categories of skin cancer in the introduction (Chunk 1). The study uses the ISIC dataset which contains malignant types (Chunk 9).

The ISIC dataset used for training includes 'benign lesions and malignant types' (Chunk 9). The literature review mentions distinguishing between 'typical picture or melanoma infected skin picture' (Chunk 3) and classifying 'harmless' (benign) lesions (Chunk 6).
Mentioned in the context of a previous study by Zafar et al. (2020) that used the ISIC-2017 and PH2 datasets for detecting epidermal tumours (Chunk 3).

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant, and specific types such as Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'Deep Learning-Based Classification of Skin Cancer'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (benign lesions vs. malignant types including BCC, Melanoma, SCC) using the ISIC dataset. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A well-known standard dataset for classifying skin cancer and segmenting lesions, consisting of annotated dermoscopic photographs (JPEG and PNG) annotated by dermatologists. It includes a wide range of skin problems, such as benign lesions and malignant types.
classes_used: Malignant types (including Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)

use.training: Used for training the NASNet and conventional CNN models, with data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, scaling, cropping) applied to enhance generalization.
use.validation: Used for assessing model effectiveness via validation accuracy and loss metrics during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, loss, precision, recall, and F1-score without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset.notes: The paper reports that NASNet surpasses traditional CNNs in classification precision, recall, and F1-score. It also states that NASNet achieves higher validation accuracy and lower loss values during training. Specific numerical metrics are not provided in the text; results are described qualitatively. Figures 3-6 show training vs. validation accuracy and loss curves, but exact values are not extracted in the text.

conclusions: NASNet outperforms conventional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in skin cancer classification, achieving higher validation accuracy, lower loss, and superior classification precision, recall, and F1-score.
The automated architecture search in NASNet optimizes feature extraction and classification accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency.
NASNet is a superior and reliable model for early skin cancer detection and diagnosis compared to traditional CNN architectures.
The study suggests that NASNet's performance makes it suitable for real-world medical applications to improve patient diagnoses.

Data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, scaling, cropping, Gaussian noise, blurring, elastic transformations, and affine distortions) were effective in minimizing class imbalance, overfitting, and maximizing model robustness.
important_remarks: The paper is a conference proceeding (ICRDICCT'25) rather than a peer-reviewed journal article, which may limit the depth of methodological scrutiny and reproducibility details.

Specific numerical performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score values) are not reported in the text; results are described qualitatively (e.g., 'higher', 'lower') with reference to figures that are not fully detailed in the provided text.

The study relies exclusively on the ISIC dataset (25,000 images) for both training and validation, lacking external validation on independent datasets to assess generalizability to different populations or imaging devices.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of NASNet over conventional CNNs; comparisons are based on descriptive metrics.

The paper does not specify the train/validation/test split ratios or the specific conventional CNN architectures (beyond general names like VGG16, ResNet) used as baselines, making exact replication difficult.
Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; it is unclear if data augmentation was applied before or after splitting the dataset, which could artificially inflate performance metrics.
The clinical relevance is speculative, claiming 'early, improved patient diagnoses' without evidence of clinical workflow integration, user studies, or comparison with dermatologist performance.
The dataset includes both benign and malignant lesions, but the specific class distribution (imbalance) is not reported, which is a critical factor in skin cancer classification studies.
